did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep stacked auto-encoder for skin cancer detection, with features extracted via convolutional neural network (CNN) and local pixel pattern-based texture features (PPBTF). The auto-encoder is trained using a proposed social bat optimisation algorithm to classify skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. It introduces a novel algorithm ('social bat optimisation dependent deep stacked auto-encoder'), describes specific methodology steps (pre-processing, feature extraction, classification), and presents original experimental results with performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) compared against existing methods, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Social bat optimisation dependent deep stacked auto-encoder for skin cancer detection
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel skin cancer detection algorithm utilizing a deep stacked auto-encoder trained by a social bat optimisation (SB) method. The approach extracts features from dermoscopy images using convolutional neural networks and local pixel pattern-based texture features (PPBTF). The SB algorithm, which integrates social ski-driver and bat algorithm concepts, is employed to optimize the classifier's weights. The model's performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics across varying training data percentages and K-fold cross-validation. The proposed method achieved a maximal accuracy of 93.38%, sensitivity of 95%, and specificity of 96%. These results demonstrate superior performance compared to existing techniques such as SVM, ANN, CNN, and RCNN + FCM.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on the detection of skin cancer in general, specifically mentioning basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and actinic keratoses as types of skin cancer in the introduction. The experimental classification task is described as binary (benign vs. melanoma or malignant vs. benign) or general skin cancer detection, but specific distinct lesion classes were not explicitly enumerated as separate experimental targets in the results section.

Mentioned as a specific type of skin cancer and a key focus for early detection. The text references classifying lesions into 'benign or melanoma' and detecting 'melanoma from the Nevus' in the context of related work and the general problem statement.

Listed as one of the four types of skin cancer in the introduction. Also mentioned in the context of related work (Mahbod et al.) that detected BCC, but not explicitly as a distinct experimental class in the proposed method's results.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer detection (binary classification of benign vs. malignant/melanoma lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'skin cancer detection' and 'classification'. It describes solving 'binary image classification issues' to distinguish between benign and malignant (melanoma) classes. The model uses a deep stacked auto-encoder as a classifier, and performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: Skin cancer dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing dermatoscopic images collected from various populations and acquired/stored by different modalities, deposited in Harvard Dataverse.

use.training: Used for training the deep stacked auto-encoder classifier with varying percentages (40%, 50%, 60%, 70%, 80%) of the data.
use.validation: Used for validation via K-fold cross-validation (specifically K=6 and K=10 mentioned in analysis).
use.testing: Used for testing performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) via K-fold cross-validation and varying training data percentages.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the proposed model by varying the number of folds (K=6 and K=10) and comparing results across different training data percentages.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics via K-fold cross-validation and varying training data percentages, with comparisons made based on these metric values rather than statistical significance.

experiment_1_varying_training_data_perce.notes: Comparative analysis based on varying training data percentages (40%, 50%, 60%, 70%, 80%). The proposed SB-based deep stacked auto-encoder achieved maximal accuracy of 93.37%, maximal sensitivity of 89.85%, and maximal specificity of 96%.

experiment_2_varying_k_fold.notes: Comparative analysis based on varying K-fold values (K=6 to K=10). The proposed SB-based deep stacked auto-encoder achieved maximal accuracy of 93.37% and maximal specificity of 96%.

architecture_used.main_model: Social bat optimisation dependent deep stacked auto-encoder

architecture_details.feature_extractor: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Local Pixel Pattern-based Texture Feature (PPBTF)
architecture_details.auto_encoder_structure: Consists of P input visible units, H hidden units, and L output visible units. Includes encoder phase with weight matrix ϖ1 and bias A1, and decoder phase with weight matrix ϖ2 and bias A2.
architecture_details.cnn_layers: Comprises convolutional (Conv), pooling (POOL), and fully connected (FC) layers. CNN features extracted from initial convolutional layer denoted as f1 with dimension 1 × 256.
architecture_details.ppbtf_description: Combination of texture features and pixel pattern-based features modified using Local Binary Pattern (LBP).

training_details.optimizer: Social Bat (SB) optimisation algorithm (integration of Social Ski-Driver (SSD) and Bat algorithm)
training_details.loss_function: Minimisation of fitness function (specific function not detailed, but accuracy/sensitivity/specificity used for evaluation)
training_details.training_schedule: Trained using SB optimisation to determine optimal weights for the stacked auto-encoder. Evaluation performed via K-fold cross-validation and varying training data percentages.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Social bat optimisation dependent deep stacked auto-encoder
preprocessing_techniques: Image enhancement (for contrast improvement and visual appearance)
Image restoration (to recover degraded images from blur and noise)
Artefact removal (specifically targeting air bubbles, ruler markings, and hair in dermoscopic images)
Removal of uninterested regions and surplus background parts
conclusions: The proposed social bat optimisation (SB) dependent deep stacked auto-encoder achieves a maximal accuracy of 93.38%, maximal sensitivity of 95%, and maximal specificity of 96% for skin cancer detection.
The SB algorithm, which integrates social ski-driver (SSD) and bat algorithm concepts, effectively updates the classifier weights and provides better performance compared to existing methods.

The proposed model outperforms comparative techniques including SVM, ANN, CNN, enhanced PSO, RCNN + FCM, and Pegah Kharazmi et al. across varying training data percentages (40%-80%) and K-fold cross-validation values (K=6 to K=10).
Performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) of the proposed model improve as the training data percentage increases from 40% to 80% and as the K-fold value increases from 6 to 10.
The study demonstrates that early detection of skin cancer using computer-aided diagnostic systems can significantly enhance patient outcomes and reduce treatment costs.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single dataset deposited in Harvard Dataverse without specifying the total sample size, class distribution, or demographic diversity, raising concerns about population bias and generalizability.

No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the internal dataset using K-fold cross-validation (K=6 and K=10), limiting evidence of robustness across different institutions or imaging devices.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the performance differences between the proposed method and comparators, relying solely on point estimates of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

Comparative baselines (SVM, ANN, CNN, etc.) show anomalously low performance in the K-fold analysis (e.g., CNN accuracy dropping to 0.16% at K=6), which suggests potential inconsistencies in experimental setup or reporting compared to the varying training percentage analysis.

The proposed method achieves a maximal accuracy of 93.38%, which is only marginally higher than the best comparator (Pegah Kharazmi et al. at 93.29% with 80% training data), indicating that the improvement may not be clinically substantial.

The feature extraction pipeline combines CNN features with Local Pixel Pattern-based Texture Features (PPBTF), but the specific integration mechanism and potential redundancy between these feature sets are not critically analyzed.
The pre-processing steps (noise/artefact removal) are described qualitatively without standardized parameters, which may introduce variability and affect reproducibility.

The study focuses on binary classification (benign vs. malignant/melanoma) rather than multi-class differentiation of specific skin cancer subtypes, limiting its direct clinical utility for differential diagnosis.
